coredata.dc:title: A fluorescence turn-on CDs-AgNPs composites for highly sensitive and selective detection of Hg2+
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Kaiyu
Sang, Yuxin
Gao, Yingdi
Sun, Qinxing
Li, Weina
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, a simple and effective fluorescence turn-on approach for highly sensitive and selective monitoring Hg2+ ions was designed by using carbon dots (CDs) and silver nanoparticles (AgNPs). It reveals that the fluorescence of CDs solution can be quenched in the presence of AgNPs through inner filter effect (IFE) and the quenched CDs-AgNPs system is turned on after addition of Hg2+ ions, which is due to higher affinity of Hg2+ and AgNPs than that of CDs and AgNPs, thus resulting the disappearance of AgNPs from the CDs-AgNPs composites and leading to the fluorescence turn-on of CDs. The developed fluorescence turn-on approach exhibited high selectivity and sensitivity for detection of Hg2+. Under the optimum experimental conditions, good linearity was achieved over the range of 100–160 μM and the limit of detection (LOD) was estimated to be 2.22×10-8 M for Hg2+. The recoveries of Hg2+ spiked in real samples ranged from 98.4% to 101.6%. Results of this study suggest that the fluorescence turn-on approach can be used to the detection of Hg2+ in real water samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescence turn-on
Hg2+ detection
Carbon dots
Silver nanoparticles
Inner filter effect

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: General procedure for detection of Hg2+
Fabrication and characterization of CDs-AgNPs composites
Mechanism for fluorescence turn-on detection of Hg2+
Determination of Hg2+ ions in real samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We gratefully acknowledge the financial support from the National Science Foundation of China (21403122) and High-level Science Foundation of Qingdao Agricultural University (6631405). We thank the Central Laboratory of QAU for the TEM test.

We gratefully acknowledge the financial support from the National Science Foundation of China ( 21403122 ) and High-level Science Foundation of Qingdao Agricultural University ( 6631405 ). We thank the Central Laboratory of QAU for the TEM test .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The UV–Vis absorption spectrum (a) and the TEM image (b) of AgNPs. Inset: particle size distribution of AgNPs.
TEM image (a); Particle size distribution (b); XRD pattern (c) and FT-IR spectrum (d) of CDs.
(a) UV–Vis absorption of CDs; (b) Fluorescence spectra of CDs with excitation wavelength from 310 nm to 390 nm by an increment of 10 nm. Inset: the photograph of CDs under UV light of 365 nm.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of different concentrations of CDs. (b) Fluorescence quenching efficiency of CDs with various amount of AgNPs. F0 represents the fluorescence intensity of original CDs and F represents the fluorescence intensity of CDs in presence of AgNPs.

(a) The normalized absorption spectrum of AgNPs (curve a) and fluorescence spectrum of CDs (curve b); (b) The zeta potential of CDs, AgNPs with and without chitosan as stabilizers; (c) The fluorescence lifetimes of CDs and CDs-AgNPs composites.

(a) Fluorescence spectra and corresponding photographs (inset) of CDs, CDs-AgNPs composites and CDs-AgNPs composites with addition of Hg2+ ions; (b) Fluorescence spectra of CDs and CDs in presence of Hg2+ ions; (c) TEM images of CDs-AgNPs composites; (d) TEM images of CDs-AgNPs composites with addition of Hg2+ ions.
(a) Fluorescence spectra of CDs-AgNPs in the presence of different concentration of Hg2+. (b) The linear plot of fluorescence spectra of the CDs-AgNPs with different concentrations of Hg2+.
Fluorescence response of CDs-AgNPs to other metal ions and to a mixture of other metal ions with Hg2+.
Schematic illustration of the mechanism for the synthesis of CDs, and the sensing mechanism of the CDs-AgNPs as turn on fluorescent sensor for the detection of Hg2+.
Comparison of different sensors for detecting Hg2+.
Recoveries of CDs-AgNPs for the determination of Hg2+ in drinking water and lake water samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: A fluorescence turn-on CDs-AgNPs composites for highly sensitive and selective detection of Hg2+

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Sciences, Qingdao Agricultural University, Qingdao 266109, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Sciences, Qingdao Agricultural University, Qingdao 266109, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food Science and Engineering, Qingdao Agricultural University, Qingdao 266109, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Food Science and Engineering, Qingdao Agricultural University, Qingdao 266109, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, a simple and effective fluorescence turn-on approach for highly sensitive and selective monitoring Hg2+ ions was designed by using carbon dots (CDs) and silver nanoparticles (AgNPs). It reveals that the fluorescence of CDs solution can be quenched in the presence of AgNPs through inner filter effect (IFE) and the quenched CDs-AgNPs system is turned on after addition of Hg2+ ions, which is due to higher affinity of Hg2+ and AgNPs than that of CDs and AgNPs, thus resulting the disappearance of AgNPs from the CDs-AgNPs composites and leading to the fluorescence turn-on of CDs. The developed fluorescence turn-on approach exhibited high selectivity and sensitivity for detection of Hg2+. Under the optimum experimental conditions, good linearity was achieved over the range of 100–160 μM and the limit of detection (LOD) was estimated to be 2.22×10-8 M for Hg2+. The recoveries of Hg2+ spiked in real samples ranged from 98.4% to 101.6%. Results of this study suggest that the fluorescence turn-on approach can be used to the detection of Hg2+ in real water samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Mercury ions (Hg2+) is one of the most toxic and universal environmental pollutants. Toxicological studies have confirmed that Hg2+ ions are not biodegradable and have a tendency to accumulate in human body, which can cause extremely serious damage to the kidneys, brain, immune system and central nervous system [1–3]. Therefore, it is an urgent need to develop convenient and low cost technologies to monitor Hg2+ ions highly selectively and sensitively in real aqueous solutions. Recently, a large number of analytical methods with relative high sensitivity have been exploited for Hg2+ detection, including atomic absorption/emission spectroscopy (AAS/AES) [4–5], solid phase microextraction-high performance liquid chromatography (SPME-HPLC) [6–7], inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) [8–9], electrochemistry [10–11] and adsorption dissolution voltammetry [12–13] etc. These instrumental techniques can provide direct and quantitative information about mercury concentrations, but always limited by time-consuming manipulations and high cost of instrumentation.

In comparison, fluorescence-based methods provide a useful alternative for detection of mercury, benefiting from the simplicity, high sensitivity and specificity, cost-effective equipment. Up till now, most reported fluorescent methods for Hg2+ ions detection are based on small organic molecules or polymeric structures, in which the fluorescence signals of the probes are always quenched due to the efficient spin–orbit coupling and non-radiation deactivation of the excited states of Hg2+ ions [14–18]. Practically, probes/sensors which lead to fluorescence enhancement upon contacting with Hg2+ ions (fluorescence turn-on probes/sensors) are preferred over their corresponding fluorescence turn-off probes/sensors mainly due to their ease detection and low background signals. Nevertheless, the reports for Hg2+ recognition based on fluorescence turn-on are rare [19–20]. Furthermore, most of the reported turn-on sensing systems always require high synthetic expertise to prepare multistep and expensive fluorescence probes with laborious purification steps. Thus, there is still enormous demand for developing efficient, highly sensitive and selective fluorescence turn-on approaches for Hg2+ with relatively simple synthetic methodology.

As a biocompatible carbon-based nanomaterial, CDs possess great potential to fabricate outstanding fluorescent probes for the monitoring of various analytes, including organic molecules, fungicides, and metal ions etc [21–23], owing to its unique properties, such as strong fluorescence, easy preparation, and good photostability. For the detection of metal ions, CDs can interact with analytes through different modes, such as fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) [24–25], inner filter effect (IFE) [26–27], and photo induced electron transfer (PET) [28–29]. By comparison, the intermolecular interaction between fluorophore and absorber is not necessary for the design of IFE, which provides a more convenient and simpler strategy to design the fluorescence sensors. AgNPs with relatively high extinction coefficient in the UV–Vis regions [30], exhibits the great potential to act as an efficient absorber in an IFE system.

In this work, a simple and effective fluorescence turn-on strategy for highly sensitive and selective detection of Hg2+ ions was designed by using CDs and AgNPs. (Scheme 1 ). Firstly, the colloidal silver nanoparticles were prepared by reduction of AgNO3 using sodium borohydride with chitosan as stabilizers and complexing agents at room temperature. CDs were fabricated via a facile one-step hydrothermal method by using citric acid as carbon sources and melamine and formaldehyde as the binary dopants. Afterwards, the fluorescence quenched CDs-AgNPs system was constructed through the IFE between CDs and AgNPs. Upon the addition of Hg2+, the quenched fluorescence of CDs-AgNPs system was turned on immediately (Scheme 1), which can be attributed to the higher affinity of Hg2+ and AgNPs than that of CDs and AgNPs. The interaction between Hg2+ and AgNPs can result in the disappearance of AgNPs and lead to the fluorescence turn-on of CDs. This fluorescence turn-on approach exhibited high selectivity and sensitivity for detection of Hg2+. Under the optimum experimental conditions, good linearity was achieved within the range of 100–160 μM and the LOD of Hg2+ was estimated to be 2.22 × 10-8 M. The recoveries of Hg2+ spiked in real samples ranged from 98.4% to 101.6%. Results of this study suggests that this fluorescence turn-on approach can be used to the detection of Hg2+ in real water samples.

All the chemicals and reagents were of analytical grade and used without further purification unless otherwise stated. Mercuric chloride (HgCl2) and silver nitrate (AgNO3) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Bio-macromolecule chitosan (viscosity 100–200 mPa·s, deacetylation degree ≥ 95%) was ordered from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Technology Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Anhydrous citric acid, melamine, formaldehyde solution (37.0–40.0%, about 14 M), sodium borohydride (NaBH4), Cu(ClO4)2·6H2O, Co(ClO4)2·6H2O, Mg(ClO4)2, Mn(ClO4)2·6H2O, Cd(ClO4)2·6H2O, Ni(ClO4)2·6H2O, Zn(ClO4)2·6H2O, Al(ClO4)3·9H2O, PdCl2, CaCl2 were purchased from Energy Chemical Technology Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China).

The morphology and microstructure were observed using a transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Hitachi HT7700). UV–Vis absorption spectra were obtained with the use of a U-3900 spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). The collection of fluorescent spectra was performed on a fluorescence spectrometer F-7000 (Hitachi, Japan). The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were collected by using a D8 advance diffractometer (Bruker, Germany). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum was recorded on a FTIR 360 spectrometer (Nicolet, Madison, USA). Fluorescence decay measurements were performed on a FLS1000 fluorescence spectrometer.

CDs were prepared by a previous reported hydrothermal method [31]. Following a typical preparation procedure, 63.0 mg (0.50 mmol) melamine and 3840.0 mg (20.0 mmol) citric acid were firstly dissolved in ultrapure water (20 mL), Then 1.12 mL (16.0 mmol) formaldehyde solution was added. The mixture was stirred for 30 min and then transferred into a 50 mL autoclave and heated at 220 °C for 10 h. After naturally cooling down to ambient temperature, the dark yellow solution was obtained and filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane. The filtrate was dialyzed against ultrapure water by using dialysis bag (1000 Da) for 24 h with renewing water every 6 h. Afterwards, the CDs powder was obtained by evaporating the solution and drying at 80 °C.

Silver nanoparticles were synthesized by reduction of AgNO3 with sodium borohydride. Bio-macromolecule chitosan was used as stabilizers and complexing agents. Typically, silver (I) (0.21 mol/L) and NaBH4 stock solution (1.26 mol/L) were all freshly prepared. Chitosan (0.20 wt%) was dissolved in 0.20 mol/L acetic acid solution. Afterwards, silver stock solution (5 mL) was added to the chitosan solution (100 mL) and the mixture was stirred for 10 min. After mixing evenly, the NaBH4 solution was added dropwise with a ratio of 1:3 to the concentration of Ag+ ions. Then the mixture was kept stirring for 30 min at room temperature to form AgNPs with a uniform size. The obtained AgNPs were kept in dark before use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: General procedure for detection of Hg2+
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the quenching effect of AgNPs and confirm the interaction between CDs and AgNPs, the fluorescence spectra of solutions containing CDs and various amounts of AgNPs were recorded. Typically, CDs solution and different amounts of AgNPs were mixed. The final concentration of CDs was 5 μg/mL and AgNPs were set to be 15, 20, 25, 30, 35, 40, 45, 50 and 55 nM. The fluorescence spectra in the range from 300 to 600 nm were recorded with the excitation wavelength of 340 nm.

For the detection of Hg2+, into a series of 5 mL colorimetric tubes, 0.5 mL of the CDs (20 µg/mL) and 1.5 mL of AgNPs (60 nM) were firstly added and mixed well. Then 200.0 μL different concentrations of Hg2+ solution was added. The mixed solutions were incubated at ambient temperature for 4 min. The fluorescence spectra were recorded between 300 and 600 nm wavelength range at the excitation wavelength of 340 nm. The slit widths of the emission and excitation were 10 nm.

Samples of lake water and drinking water were collected from Qingdao Agricultural University campus and the water samples were firstly filtered through a 0.2 μm membrane to remove any suspensions. Then a series of samples by spiking the collected water with standard solutions of Hg2+ were prepared. These spiked samples were added to a solution of CDs-AgNPs. The resulting solutions were incubated for 4 min before measuring the fluorescence spectra.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication and characterization of CDs-AgNPs composites
ce:section.ce:para: Scheme 1 illustrates the fabrication and application of CDs-AgNPs composites as a fluorescence turn-on sensor for highly selective and sensitive detection of Hg2+ ions. Firstly, the colloidal silver nanoparticles were prepared by reduction of AgNO3 using sodium borohydride with chitosan as stabilizers and complexing agents at room temperature. As displayed in Fig. 1 a, the formation of metallic AgNPs is confirmed by the appearance of the surface plasmon resonance (SPR) band at 398 nm in the UV–Vis spectra. The morphological structures of AgNPs were characterized by TEM (Fig. 1b and Figure S1), which shows that the AgNPs are nearly spherical and well-dispersed with diameters of 14 nm. The concentration of the prepared AgNPs was calculated to be 60 nM according to the mean diameter of AgNPs.

CDs were fabricated via a facile one-step hydrothermal method using citric acid as major carbon source, melamine as the nitrogen source and formaldehyde to modify the nitrogen chemical state of CDs. TEM was used to characterize the morphology. As displayed in Fig. 2 a, the CDs are monodispersed and quasi spherical in shape. The diameters are estimated in the range of 2–7 nm and the average particle size are 5 nm (Fig. 2b). The XRD pattern of the CDs in Fig. 2c exhibits a broad diffraction peak centered at around 21°, which is ascribed to the disordered carbon atoms. FTIR spectra was employed to further characterize the surface functional groups of the CDs. As shown in Fig. 2d, a broad absorption peak at 3444 cm−1 is assigned to N-H/O-H stretching vibration. The absorption bands at 2965 cm−1, 1671 cm−1, 1574 cm−1, 1452 cm−1 and 1191 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibration of CH, CO, CC, COO and CO/CN, respectively [32–34]. The FTIR spectrum demonstrates that multiple functional groups, including OH, COOH, NH2, etc., exist on the surface of CDs. These functionalities make the CDs of high hydrophilicity and possessing excellent interaction sites for further applications.

The UV–Vis absorbance spectrum (Fig. 3 a) of CDs exhibits two absorption peaks at 280 nm and 320 nm, which are attributed to π-π* transition of sp2 conjugated extensions and n-π* transition of the presence of amine/carbonyl functional groups on the surface, respectively [35]. The excitation wavelength-dependent emission behavior was also observed. With the excitation wavelength increasing from 310 to 390 nm, the emission peak red-shifted from 416 to 458 nm and the peak fluorescence at 422 nm was obtained at excitation wavelength of 340 nm (Figure 3b). This excitation-dependent emission property may be attributed to the various surface state and different energy levels related to the surface functional groups [36]. The aqueous solution of CDs is colorless and nearly transparent under visible light, and is blue under 365 nm UV irradiation (Figure 3b inset). The fluorescence decay curve of CDs (Figure S2) shows three life-time components, including 1.20 ns (10.23%), 4.78 ns (58.05%) and 14.78 ns (31.72%), and the calculated average lifetime was 7.59 ns.

To build up a highly sensitive and selective fluorescence turn-on strategy for Hg2+ ions detection, the blue-emission CDs and AgNPs composites were further constructed. As we all know that the concentration of CDs and AgNPs has tremendous impacts on the recognition performance to Hg2+. We firstly investigated the concentration of CDs changed from 1 to 10 μg mL−1, and found that the fluorescence intensity at 422 nm increased gradually, while a shoulder peak at 390 nm appeared when the concentration lowered than 5 μg mL−1 (Fig. 4 a). Comprehensively considering the stability and recognition ability of CDs, the concentration 5 μg mL−1 was chosen as the experimental optimal conditions to construct the composites.

To further confirm the fluorescence property of CDs in the presence of AgNPs and explore the optimum concentration of AgNPs, the fluorescence emission spectra were collected and displayed in Fig. 4b. As increasing the concentration of AgNPs from 15.0 to 55.0 nM, the fluorescence intensity of CDs is decreased gradually and the maximum quenching is observed when the concentration of AgNPs is up to 45.0 nM. Therefore, the concentration 45.0 nM was applied in the further experiments to fabricate CDs-AgNPs composites for fluorescence turn-on detection of Hg2+ ions.

In order to investigate the underlying mechanism of fluorescence quenching of CDs-AgNPs composites, the absorption band of AgNPs and the fluorescence spectrum of CDs were firstly discussed. It is revealed in Fig. 5 a that the emission spectrum of CDs (curve b) could over-lap the absorption spectrum of AgNPs (curve a) at large extent, which provides an indispensable condition for inner filter effect (IFE) or fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) process. The zeta potential of CDs and AgNPs were also detected. As depicted in Fig. 5b, the CDs were negatively charged (ζ = -0.354), while AgNPs with chitosan as stabilizers were positively charged (ζ = 26.8). For comparison, AgNPs without chitosan protection were also synthesized and the zeta potential was tested to be −3.65. The presence of chitosan makes AgNPs positively charged and thus exhibiting electrostatic attraction towards CDs. Meanwhile, the fluorescence lifetime of CDs before and after the addition of AgNPs were measured. As shown in Fig. 5c, the fluorescence lifetime of CDs in the absence and presence of AgNPs is 7.59 ns and 7.55 ns, respectively. There are no obvious changes in the fluorescence lifetime. It is well known that fluorescence lifetime is mostly unaffected by IFE but would be substantially changed by an efficient FRET process. So the fluorescence quenching effect of CDs by AgNPs is ascribed to IFE not the FRET. As shown in Figure S3, the stability of CDs-AgNPs composites has also been tested. After keeping for 10 days, the the fluorescence intensity and fluorescence image under 365 nm UV light showed no obvious change. As adding Hg2+ ions, the fluorescence turn-on and the fluorescence intensity recovered within 60 s.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism for fluorescence turn-on detection of Hg2+
ce:section.ce:para: As displayed in Scheme 1 and Fig. 6 a, the fluorescence intensity of CDs-AgNPs is completely recovered after addition of Hg2+ ions. Whereas, there is no obvious difference for the fluorescence intensity of our pure CDs with and without Hg2+ ions (Fig. 6b), revealing that Hg2+ cannot directly interact with our CDs. These results demonstrate that CDs-AgNPs composites possess excellent fluorescence turn-on effect for Hg2+ ions. According to previous work [37], Hg2+ ions with the standard potential of 0.85 V (Hg2+/Hg) could interact with AgNPs (0.8 V, Ag+/Ag) through redox reaction, resulting in the disappearance of AgNPs. As demonstrated by the TEM images (Fig. 6c, 6d), the coexistence of AgNPs and CDs can be observed and AgNPs are all surrounded by CDs in the CDs-AgNPs composites. After the addition of Hg2+, the AgNPs disappeared, leaving only dispersed CDs. UV–Vis absorption spectra was also carried out to further confirm this phenomenon. As seen from Figure S4, the SPR peak of AgNPs at 398 nm disappeared after adding Hg2+ ions, which suggests the interaction between AgNPs and Hg2+. Therefore, Hg2+ can interact with AgNPs and induce the disappearance of AgNPs, and then redundant AgNPs quench the fluorescence of CDs. Thus, an effective fluorescence turn-on sensing platform was developed for the detection of Hg2+ ions, with the AgNPs serving as the Hg2+ recognition component and the CDs acting as the reference fluorophore.

Experimental parameters are extremely important in the detection process. In this sensing system, the incubation time was optimized for fluorescence turn-on detection. F/F0 is described as the fluorescence quenching of CDs aqueous solutions in the presence and absence of AgNPs. F0 represents the fluorescence intensity of original CDs. F1/F0 is used for illustrating the fluorescence enhancement efficiency, where F represents the fluorescence intensity of CDs in presence of AgNPs and F1 represents the fluorescence intensity of CDs-AgNPs composites with the addition of Hg2+.

The suitable incubation time for CDs-AgNPs composites and reaction with Hg2+ were both investigated. As shown in Figure S5a and S5b, the F/F0 and F1/F0 values gradually decrease and increase respectively as the incubation time increasing. And the CDs-AgNPs composites and Hg2+ reaction system reached equilibrium states after 10 s and 240 s respectively. As a result, 10 s was chosen as the incubation time of CDs-AgNPs composites and the time of detection of Hg2+ was set to be 240 s.

The detection of CDs-AgNPs composites to Hg2+ was investigated by measuring the fluorescence spectra of the composites upon addition of Hg2+ at various concentrations. As can be seen from Fig. 7 a, under the optimal experimental conditions, the fluorescence intensity was gradually enhanced with an increase in the concentration of Hg2+, revealing an excellent fluorescence turn-on effect. Accompanied with the concentration of Hg2+ ions ranging from 100 to 160 μM, the fluorescence intensity of CDs reached maximum at 422 nm. A linear calibration curve between fluorescence intensity of CDs at 422 nm and the concentration of Hg2+ in the range from 100 to 160 μM exhibited an excellent linear relationship with the correlation coefficients R = 0.9992. The limit of detection (LOD) was measured to be as low as 2.22 × 10-8 M based on three times the standard deviation rule. The results represent the availability of this fluorescence turn-on method for the detection of mercury ions. Table 1 shows the detection limit of Hg2+ using different materials through various methods of detection, as seen, the proposed sensor displayeda low detection limit.

To examine the selectivity of the CDs-AgNPs composites for Hg2+, the influence of various metal ions, including Al3+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Pd2+, Zn2+ and Ca2+, on the fluorescent turn-on detection of Hg2+ were systematically investigated. Firstly, the individual metal ions were added to the solution of CDs-AgNPs. Fig. 8 revealed that the fluorescence intensity of CDs-AgNPs showed no obvious enhance by addition of these metal ions. Then Hg2+ ions were added to the above mentioned systems, whereas addition of the Hg2+ ions dramatically enhanced the fluorescence intensity of CDs. It can be obviously found that even the interfering substance existed, CDs-AgNPs system still exhibited the same response to Hg2+. These results demonstrated that this fluorescence turn-on system hold excellent specificity and anti-interference capability for Hg2+ detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Hg2+ ions in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the practical application of this fluorescence turn-on system on the determination of Hg2+ ions, drinking water and lake water samples from the Hongzi Lake in Qingdao Agricultural University were collected. The water samples were firstly filtered through a 0.2 μm membrane to remove any suspensions and then were spiked with Hg2+ at various concentrations. Finally, the spiked Hg2+ ions were recovered by this fluorescence turn-on method. The analytical results are displayed in Table 2 . The recoveries for the selective detection of Hg2+ are in the range from 98.4% to 101.6%. The relative standard deviations (RSD) are lower than 1.24%. Despite the complicated components in the real samples which may interfere with the detections, the spiked Hg2+ can be detected with accuracy. These results suggest that the fluorescence turn-on method has great potential for the sensing of Hg2+ ions in environmental samples.

In conclusion, a highly sensitive and selective fluorescence turn-on sensing platform for the detection of Hg2+ ions based on CDs-AgNPs composites was developed. The blue emission CDs were prepared through a facile one-step hydrothermal treatment, which could be easily quenched by AgNPs through IFE. After introducing of Hg2+ ions, the fluorescence of CDs was turned on due to the interaction between Hg2+ ions and AgNPs. This fluorescence turn-on sensing platform had an excellent liner relationship from 100 to 160 μM and the LOD was down to 2.22 × 10-8 M. Moreover, the satisfactory accuracy was also obtained when this system was applied to real samples. These results revealed a promising application of this fluorescence turn-on sensing platform in Hg2+ monitoring.

Kaiyu Zhang: Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing - original draft. Yuxin Sang: Methodology, Data curation. Yingdi Gao: Investigation, Formal analysis. Qinxing Sun: Formal analysis, Investigation. Weina Li: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the financial support from the National Science Foundation of China (21403122) and High-level Science Foundation of Qingdao Agricultural University (6631405). We thank the Central Laboratory of QAU for the TEM test.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2021.120281.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ecological effects, transport, and fate of mercury: a general review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. W. Boening. Ecological effects, transport, and fate of mercury: a general review. Chemosphere. 2000, 40, 1335-1351.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: BODIPY-derived polymeric chemosensor appended with thiosemicarbazone units for the simultaneous detection and separation of Hg (II) ions in pure aqueous media

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: U. Haldar, H. I. Lee. BODIPY-derived polymeric chemosensor appended with thiosemicarbazone units for the simultaneous detection and separation of Hg (II) ions in pure aqueous media. ACS Appl. Mater. Inter. 2019, 11, 13685-13693.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A competitive immunochromatographic assay based on a novel probe for the detection of mercury (II) ions in water samples

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Zhou, Y. Y. Zhang, F. G. Pan, Y. S. Li, S. Lu, H. L. Ren, Q. F. Shen, Z. H. Li, J. H. Zhang, Q. J. Chen, Z. H. Liu. A competitive immunochromatographic assay based on a novel probe for the detection of mercury (II) ions in water samples. Biosens. Bioelectron. 2010, 25, 2534-2538.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synthesis and characterization of a fluorescent polymer containing 2,6-bis(2-thienyl) pyridine moieties as a highly efficient sensor for Pd2+ detection

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B. Liu, Y. Bao, F. Du, H. Wang, J. Tian, R. Bai. Synthesis and characterization of a fluorescent polymer containing 2,6-bis(2-thienyl) pyridine moieties as a highly efficient sensor for Pd2+ detection. Chem. Commun. 2011, 47, 1731-1733.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Microplasma source based on a dielectric barrier discharge for the determination of mercury by atomic emission spectrometry

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. L. Zhu, G. C. Y. Chan, S. J. Ray, X. R. Zhang, G. M. Hieftje. Microplasma source based on a dielectric barrier discharge for the determination of mercury by atomic emission spectrometry. Anal. Chem. 2008, 80, 8622-8627.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A new approach for simultaneous determination of Co (II), Ni (II), Cu (II) and Pd (II) using 2-thiophenaldehyde-3-thiosemicarbazone as reagent by solid phase microextraction-high performance liquid chromatography

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: V. Kaur, J. S. Aulakh, A. K. Malik. A new approach for simultaneous determination of Co (II), Ni (II), Cu (II) and Pd (II) using 2-thiophenaldehyde-3-thiosemicarbazone as reagent by solid phase microextraction-high performance liquid chromatography. Anal. Chim. Acta. 2007, 603, 44-50.

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: X. Y Cui., Z. Y. Gu, D. Q. Jiang, Y. Li, H. F. Wang, X. P. Yan. In situ hydrothermal growth of metal-organic framework 199 films on stainless steel fibers for solid-phase microextraction of gaseous benzene homologues. Anal. Chem. 2009, 81, 9771-9777.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Determination of ultra-trace Pt, Pd and Rh in seawater using an off-line pre-concentration method and inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Liu, X. L. Gao, L. Li, C. T. A. Chen, Q. G. Xing. Determination of ultra-trace Pt, Pd and Rh in seawater using an off-line pre-concentration method and inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry. Chemosphere. 2018, 212, 429-437.
